summary: DeVault argues that modern copyright law, particularly as extended through Disney's lobbying, has effectively stolen the public domain from the American public. Using Disney's Alice in Wonderland as a case study, he highlights the irony that Disney built its classic film on a public domain work but now prevents others from doing the same with their adaptation until 2046. He notes that nearly all creators of the film are dead, yet copyright persists for decades more. The original purpose of copyright—incentivizing creation with a short exclusivity period before enriching the public domain—has been perverted into a rent-seeking system that benefits corporations at the public's expense.
key_points: Disney's Alice in Wonderland is itself a derivative work based on Lewis Carroll's 1865 public domain novel, yet Disney's version won't enter the public domain until 2046

Under modern copyright law (which Disney lobbied for), Carroll's novel would not have been in the public domain when Disney made their film—they released it just 17 years before Carroll's work would have entered public domain under current rules
Nearly every creator credited on the 1951 film is now dead, yet copyright protection continues for another 26 years with no living creators to benefit
The original US copyright term was 14 years (renewable to 28), designed to incentivize creation then enrich the public domain—modern terms have inverted this purpose into corporate rent-seeking
money_quotes: It has been stolen from those generations, as part of the theft of one of these generations' greatest treasures: the public domain.
No one can create derivative works of their own without receiving permission from and paying a tithe to Disney.

If modern-day copyright law, bought and paid for by Disney, had been in force at the time Alice in Wonderland was made, they would have released their film 17 years before Carroll's novel entered the public domain.
The copyright remains in force, though no creators remain to enjoy its privileges.
The obscene copyright terms we're faced with today have robbed the American public of its national heritage.
Any work made today will not enter the public domain during the lifetimes of any of its contemporaries, let alone soon enough for those contemporaries to do anything with it.
A system designed to incentivize creation has become a system which incentivises the opposite: rent seeking.
key_insight: Modern copyright law has been corrupted from a system that briefly rewards creators before enriching the public domain into a perpetual rent-seeking mechanism that benefits corporations long after every creator is dead.
